sn1.71:1.1: At Sāvatthī. Standing to one side, that devata addressed the Blessed One in verse:
sn1.71:2.1:
v.223 "Having slain what does one sleep soundly?
Having slain what does one not sorrow?
What is the one thing, O Gotama,
Whose killing you approve?"[n.127] Se, Ee2 jhatva is certainly the correct reading, chetva in Be and Ee1 a normalization. The gloss in Spk, vadhitva, supports jhatva, and G-Dhp 288–89 has jatva, the Gandharī Prakrit counterpart. See Brough, Gandhari Dharmapada, pp. 164, 265–66. Jhatva is also found in the SS reading of v. 94b.
sn1.71:3.1:
v.224 "Having slain anger, one sleeps soundly;
Having slain anger, one does not sorrow;
The killing of anger, O devata,
With its poisoned root and honeyed tip:
This is the killing the noble ones praise,
For having slain that, one does not sorrow."[n.128] Spk: Anger has a poisoned root (visamūla) because it results in suffering. It has a honeyed tip (madhuragga) because pleasure arises when one returns anger with anger, abuse with abuse, or a blow with a blow.
